eli3: Language models are trained on text and can only output text. Tool use gives them superpowers: the ability to actually do things — run code, search the web, look up a database, send an email. The model decides when to use a tool and what inputs to give it, just like deciding which app to open on your phone.

The model doesn't run the tool itself — it says 'please call calculator(2+2)' and the framework does it, then shows the model the result '4'. The model uses that result to continue its answer. This way, the model can handle tasks it couldn't do with text alone.

Tool use is what turns a chatbot into an agent. Without tools, a model can only recall what it was trained on. With tools, it can look things up, compute things, and take actions in the real world — making it genuinely useful for tasks that need current or precise information.

whenWhy.title: Accessing up-to-date or external information
whenWhy.body: LLMs have a training cutoff and no access to live data. Tool use enables: web search for current news/prices, database queries for customer records, API calls for weather/stock data. Without tools, the model hallucinates or admits ignorance. With a search tool, it can retrieve authoritative, current information and ground its response in real data. This is the most common reason to add tool use to a production LLM application.

whenWhy.title: Precise computation and code execution
whenWhy.body: LLMs are unreliable at arithmetic, symbolic math, and structured data manipulation. Tool use enables: calling a Python interpreter for numerical computation, running SQL queries for aggregations, calling a calculator for financial math. Instead of asking the LLM to compute compound interest (error-prone), give it a code execution tool and let it write and run the calculation. The tool returns exact results; the LLM synthesises them into a natural language answer.

whenWhy.title: Taking actions in external systems
whenWhy.body: Beyond information retrieval, tools enable write operations: creating calendar events, sending emails, filing tickets, deploying code, modifying database records. These are the 'effectors' that make an LLM an agent capable of autonomous task completion. The LLM decides when and how to call these tools; the framework executes them. This requires careful authorization controls (what tools are available, with what permissions) and confirmation workflows for irreversible actions.

deepDiveIntro: Tool use is the mechanism by which an LLM reaches beyond its static training data to interact with external systems — retrieving live information, executing code, and taking real-world actions. Rather than generating a final text answer in a single forward pass, a tool-use-enabled model can pause its generation, emit a structured tool invocation, receive the result, and then continue generating with the new information incorporated. This transforms the LLM from a knowledge lookup system into an active computational agent capable of multi-step reasoning grounded in real data.

Tool use in LLMs was formalised by Toolformer (Schick et al. 2023): the model is trained to insert API calls inline in its generation, replacing the call with the returned value before continuing generation. This showed that LLMs could learn when and how to use tools from a relatively small amount of self-supervised training data. Modern approaches (OpenAI function calling, Anthropic tool use) move the tool call detection out of the generation stream and into a structured output format — the LLM emits a JSON object specifying the tool name and arguments, the framework validates and executes it, and the result is injected back as a structured message. This separation of description (JSON schema), invocation (structured output), and execution (framework layer) enables type-safe, validated tool calling at scale. [1]

deepDive.heading: Tool Definition via JSON Schema
deepDive.body: Every tool is described by a JSON schema: name (string), description (natural language — this is what the LLM reads to decide when to use the tool), and parameters (JSON Schema object with property names, types, descriptions, and required fields). The description is the most important field: vague descriptions lead to misuse; precise descriptions (including when NOT to use the tool) dramatically improve tool selection accuracy. Best practice: include examples in the description, specify the output format, and list known failure modes. [1]

deepDive.heading: Parallel vs Sequential Tool Calls
deepDive.body: OpenAI and Anthropic APIs support parallel tool calling: the LLM emits multiple tool calls in a single response (a JSON array). The framework can execute them concurrently, reducing latency. Example: an agent needing stock prices for 5 tickers can call get_stock_price() 5 times in parallel rather than sequentially. The framework must handle partial failures (some calls succeed, some fail) and return all results before the LLM continues. Sequential tool calls are used when step N depends on the output of step N-1; the LLM controls this by making a single tool call and waiting for the result before deciding on the next. [1]

deepDive.heading: Tool Result Injection and Context Management
deepDive.body: Tool results are injected back into the conversation as a 'tool' role message (Anthropic) or function role message (OpenAI). The message includes the tool call ID (to match response to request), the tool name, and the result (string). For large tool outputs (e.g., a 50-page document), the result must be truncated or summarised before injection — injecting raw large results causes context overflow and degrades LLM performance. Strategies: (1) truncate to first N tokens; (2) extract a structured summary; (3) store in a vector DB and retrieve relevant chunks. [2]

deepDive.heading: Tool Error Handling and Retry Strategies
deepDive.body: Tool failures are inevitable in production: APIs go down, rate limits are hit, queries return empty results, and the LLM occasionally generates invalid argument values. The correct pattern is to always return errors as tool result messages — never raise uncaught exceptions that crash the agent loop. Error messages should be informative and actionable: 'Invalid date format: expected YYYY-MM-DD, received 04/12/2026' gives the LLM enough context to self-correct on the next call. Implement a per-tool retry budget (typically 2–3 attempts) tracked by the framework, not the LLM. Distinguish retryable errors (network timeout, rate limit) from terminal errors (invalid credentials, resource not found) and handle them differently. For retryable errors, exponential backoff with jitter prevents thundering-herd problems. After the retry budget is exhausted, surface a structured failure to the caller with the full error context, enabling human-in-the-loop recovery or graceful degradation. [1]

takeaways: Tool use gives LLMs access to external systems (search, code execution, databases, APIs) beyond their training data. [1]
Tools are defined via JSON schemas; the description field is critical for the LLM to know when and how to call the tool. [1]
Parallel tool calling (multiple tools in one response) reduces latency for independent sub-tasks. [1]
Tool results are injected back as structured messages; large results must be truncated or summarised. [2]
Toolformer (2023) demonstrated self-supervised learning of tool use; modern APIs externalise this into structured JSON. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you prevent the LLM from calling tools it shouldn't (e.g., destructive operations)?
edgeCases.a: Layers of defence: (1) Tool availability — only expose tools the agent needs for the current task; don't give a read-only agent write tools; (2) Confirmation workflows — for irreversible actions (delete, send, pay), insert a human-approval step before execution; the agent proposes the action, a human confirms; (3) Parameter validation — validate all tool parameters server-side before execution, independent of the LLM's output; (4) Least-privilege API keys — tool implementations should use credentials with the minimum necessary permissions; (5) Structured output validation — use JSON Schema validation to reject malformed tool calls before they reach the tool implementation.

edgeCases.q: What happens when a tool call returns an error?
edgeCases.a: Return the error message as the tool result — do not throw an exception that crashes the agent loop. The LLM reads the error message and can: (1) retry with corrected parameters (e.g., if the error was 'invalid date format', reformat the date); (2) try an alternative tool that achieves the same goal; (3) inform the user that the tool failed. Error messages should be informative: 'File not found: /tmp/data.csv' is actionable; a generic 'Error 500' is not. Include the original parameters in the error message so the LLM can debug its own call. Set a per-tool retry limit (max 2-3 retries) to prevent infinite retry loops.

sources.label: Schick et al. (2023) — Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools, NeurIPS 2023
Anthropic Tool Use Documentation — official API reference
